error.signature: AI tells a tattooed traveler they can use any Japanese onsen, sento, public pool, gym, or beach in Japan
error.regex: (japan.*tattoo|onsen.*tattoo|sento.*tattoo|タトゥー.*温泉|刺青|入れ墨|irezumi|japanese pool.*tattoo|gym japan tattoo)

verdict.summary: Many Japanese onsen, sento (public baths), gyms, pools, and beaches refuse entry to people with visible tattoos — associated historically with the yakuza. This is a private-venue policy, not criminal law. Roughly 40% of onsen nationwide ban tattoos outright; major exceptions exist (Kinosaki, Beppu, urban 'tattoo-friendly' lists) and small tattoos can be covered with patches. Japan Tourism Agency has pushed for more tolerance since the 2019 Rugby World Cup, but compliance is mixed.

dead_ends.action: Walk into a random onsen in a resort area with a visible sleeve tattoo and expect standard entry
dead_ends.why_fails: Visible tattoo = refusal at reception ('お断り' signage at the entrance is common). The staff may not ask about the meaning or size; any visible ink triggers the blanket policy. Tourists report feeling singled out; the policy is not individual but blanket across the venue.

dead_ends.action: Assume gym memberships include pool/spa access even with tattoos
dead_ends.why_fails: Konami, Tipness, Central Sports, and other major Japanese gym chains typically enforce the same no-tattoo policy in pools/saunas. Gym membership contracts often list it explicitly. The policy extends to some hotel gyms and onsen-attached hotels.

dead_ends.action: Try to 'cover up' a full back tattoo with small bandages to get into a ryokan onsen
dead_ends.why_fails: Cover-up tolerance applies only to small tattoos (palm-size or smaller) — larger tattoos are not considered concealable and will be refused regardless of effort. Onsen-grade bandages sold for this purpose work for small tattoos; larger pieces need tattoo-friendly venues.

workarounds.action: Use tattoo-friendly lists: Japan Tattoo-Friendly Onsen Map (tattoo-friendly.jp), Bōsō Kinosaki Onsen (all 7 baths), Beppu Hells area, Nozawa Onsen, most Kawaguchiko ryokans. Hotel-attached private baths (kashikiri buro 貸切風呂) often allow tattoos — book in advance.
For small tattoos: buy tattoo cover stickers (タトゥーシール) at Don Quijote, Tokyu Hands, or onsen souvenir shops. They're waterproof and designed for baths. Works only for ≤10cm tattoos.
workarounds.tradeoff: Only effective for small / low-contrast pieces.

workarounds.action: Book private rooms with in-room onsen (kashikiri) or family-style baths at ryokan. These bypass the public policy entirely. Slightly more expensive but common at mid-to-high-end ryokan.
